mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Latvia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — northern Europe, on the Baltic Sea. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Latvia?
mapQuestions.answer: Riga — the largest city in the three Baltic states.

mapQuestions.question: Which countries border Latvia?
mapQuestions.answer: Estonia to the north, Lithuania to the south, Russia and Belarus to the east.

mapQuestions.question: What language do Latvians speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Latvian — one of only two surviving Baltic languages (the other is Lithuanian). It's older than English or German.

mapQuestions.question: What is the famous golden material found on Baltic beaches in Latvia?
mapQuestions.answer: Amber — fossilised tree resin millions of years old, often containing trapped insects.

foods.name: Rye bread (rupjmaize)
foods.description: Dense dark Latvian rye — a staple for centuries. Sweet, sour, and chewy. Latvians say "no meal is complete without rye bread".

foods.name: Kefir
foods.description: A drinkable yoghurt eaten with sweet or savoury food. Sometimes paired with cold beetroot soup.

foods.name: Aukstā zupa
foods.description: Cold pink beetroot soup with kefir, cucumber, dill, and boiled egg. Eaten in summer. Looks like a milkshake.

foods.name: Smoked sprats
foods.description: Tiny Baltic fish, smoked and tinned. A Latvian export — millions of tins a year. Eaten on bread.

foods.name: Pīrāgi
foods.description: Small filled rolls — usually with bacon and onion. Latvian Christmas and party food.

foods.name: Buckwheat porridge
foods.description: Roasted buckwheat groats — eaten as porridge with milk for breakfast or with meat for dinner.

scavengerItems.label: A medieval castle or old fortress wall
A forest reaching right into town (Latvia is 50%+ forest)
A stork nest on a chimney or telegraph pole
Someone cycling in the rain like it's normal
A traditional Latvian-pattern textile or knit jumper
A bakery with sweet sourdough rye bread
A choir or folk-singing group rehearsing
A wooden building older than your country
Someone foraging mushrooms or berries in a forest
A statue or memorial to Latvian independence

geoMatches.description: The capital. Beautiful Art Nouveau architecture (Riga has more than any city in Europe), medieval Old Town, and the Daugava River.
A long Baltic beach resort just outside Riga. White sand, pine forests, and grand wooden 1900s holiday villas.
A pretty town in the Gauja River valley, with castle ruins, ziplines, and a bobsled track from the 1980 Olympics.
A wild windswept point where the Baltic Sea meets the Gulf of Riga. Home to the last Livonian fishing villages.
Latvia's biggest park, with sandstone cliffs, river valleys, and forests stretching for over 90 km.

senses.smell: Pine forest, dark rye bread, smoked fish, wet leaves, Baltic salt air…
senses.hear: Latvian choirs (singing is a national obsession), rain on cobblestones, church bells, "Sveiki!"…
senses.taste: Sour rye bread with butter, smoky sprats, sweet beetroot, kefir, wild forest berries…
senses.feel: Smooth amber beads, soft moss, cold Baltic water, rough wool socks…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is amber found on Latvian beaches?
stories.body: Around 50 million years ago, vast pine forests grew along what is now the Baltic Sea. When the trees were damaged they oozed sticky resin, which dripped down and fossilised over millions of years into amber. Storms wash chunks of this fossil resin onto Baltic beaches even today. Sometimes a piece of amber has a perfectly preserved insect trapped inside — a fly or beetle that got stuck in the resin tens of millions of years ago. Latvian and Lithuanian markets sell amber jewellery for very reasonable prices. Look for honey-coloured pieces with insects inside — those are worth more.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Imagine being trapped in amber and someone finds you 50 million years later. What would they learn about how you lived?

stories.question: Why is singing such a big deal in Latvia?
stories.body: Latvia has 2 million people but stages a "Song and Dance Festival" every five years with 40,000+ choir singers and dancers performing together. It's been happening since 1873 and is on UNESCO's list of intangible heritage. Singing was a huge part of Latvia's independence movement in 1987-1991 — when Soviet tanks tried to take Riga in 1991, Latvians (and Estonians, Lithuanians) gathered in their hundreds of thousands and SANG, building barricades around the parliament. It was called the "Singing Revolution" and it worked — they got independence peacefully.

stories.question: Why does Riga have so many Art Nouveau buildings?
stories.body: Around 1900, Riga was a booming Russian Empire port and the third-richest city in the empire. Rich merchants wanted impressive homes, and a young architect Mikhail Eisenstein (father of the famous Soviet filmmaker) had just discovered the new Art Nouveau style coming out of Vienna and Paris. He went wild — covering Riga's new apartment buildings with carved faces, animals, dragons, women's heads, flowers in stone. Today around one-third of central Riga is Art Nouveau — more than anywhere else in Europe. Just walking around the streets is like walking through a museum.

stories.question: What does it mean to "occupy" a country, and what happened to Latvia?
stories.body: Latvia became independent for the first time in 1918, after centuries under Germans, Swedes, Poles, and Russians. Independence lasted just 22 years. In 1940 the Soviet Union invaded and "annexed" Latvia (took it as part of the USSR). Then Nazi Germany invaded in 1941 — for three terrible years Latvia was occupied by the Nazis. Then the Soviets came back in 1944 and ruled Latvia for another 45 years. About 200,000 Latvians (out of 2 million) were killed or deported to Siberia during this period. Latvia only became free again in 1991, with the Singing Revolution.

stories.question: Why is foraging in the forest still such a Latvian habit?
stories.body: Latvia is one of Europe's most forested countries — over 50% is forest. For centuries, Latvians have foraged for mushrooms, berries, herbs, and pine nuts. During the Soviet era, food shortages made foraging even more important, and many families kept their secret forest spots. Today the tradition is still strong: in autumn, whole families head into the woods on weekends with baskets. Chanterelles, porcini, blueberries, lingonberries, cranberries — all free for the picking. There's no English equivalent for the Latvian word "klusums" — the deep silence of a Latvian forest. Latvians treasure it.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Most of us never eat food we found ourselves. Does that change our relationship with food? If so, how?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Latvia so far?
Have you spotted any amber jewellery you liked?
Latvia is small — about 2 million people. What do you notice about small countries?
Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia — would you have known these countries existed before this trip?
What's one Latvian habit you'd like to bring home?
A "Singing Revolution" overthrew the Soviet Union. Is there any way you can imagine a real change happening through music or song today?

wordSearch.hint: Latvian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Latvia has one of the highest stork densities in Europe — nests on chimneys, poles, even traffic lights.

animals.name: Eurasian beaver
animals.description: Very common in Latvian rivers — look for dams and gnawed trees.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: In forests and increasingly close to farms. Often seen at dawn.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In Gauja National Park and the eastern forests.

animals.name: Eurasian elk (moose)
animals.description: Common in Latvian forests — about 25,000 in the country.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Slowly returning from Russia. Maybe 10–15 wandering bears, mostly in the east.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Around 500 wolves in Latvia's deep forests. Hunted in winter under strict quotas.
